notes: Founding year not stated. Rescue committee concluded saving the parish was impossible; bishop closed it unilaterally.
Last Lithuanian priest died; building sold by diocese ~1987 to a Mexican Catholic congregation.
Bridgeport. Founding-year conflict: 1892 parish, 1902 building, 1909 also cited. Demolition-year conflict: 1990 vs 1992. Letter campaign to cardinal and Pope failed.
One of the first Chicago Lithuanian churches to close; demolished without recorded resistance.
Pilsen/Brighton Park area. Closed ~1985-1990 (1987 in Phase 1); merged with Šv. Pauliaus. Distinct from Manhattan NY Aušros Vartų.
Marquette Park/Brighton Park area (probable). Closed pre-2013; building fate not specified; 10 stained-glass windows repatriated to Lithuania.
East Side. Second-oldest Lithuanian church in Chicago; closed 1987 as parishioners became Spanish-speaking.
18th Street/Pilsen. Founded ~1900; closed 2016 under Renew My Church. Distinct from Detroit/Southfield RC and Scranton LNCC Dievo Apvaizdos. 2023 source tension flagged.
Back of the Yards/Town of Lake. Extinguished as a parish by Cupich decree Dec 10 2020; building survives as a worship center under demolition threat. Probable single parish (D1/D4 duplicate flagged).
Historical reference only; building may remain but identity uncertain.
Closed 1989 and demolished; Lithuanica Avenue neighborhood.
Closed 1998; demolition-year conflict 2001 vs 2007; on National Register, which gave no protection.
Closed 1947 by a bishop who disliked ethnic parishes; building status unknown.
Closed June 2008, merged into St. Michael Avon; favorable post-closure asset split negotiated but closure could not be prevented.
Closed (date unknown); converted to apartments with Lithuanian facade inscriptions preserved as a landmark requirement.
Closed July 1 2002; archdiocese appropriated all property including a lakeside summer estate. Contrast with the LNCC parish in the same city/year.
Unnamed Lithuanian church; abandoned by 2017, razed by 2023; only a cornerstone survives.
Closed; converted to an ordinary residential house; Lithuanian signs removed. Boston suburb, kept separate from Boston proper.

Closed July 1 2008, merged into Šv. Jono. Solvent, debt-free, with paid-off school; financial health did not save it. Rescue committee, canon lawyer, Vatican appeal all failed. A 2010-03-09 Draugas article (corrected 2010-03-24) records this parish's ~$1M pre-closure renovation and Vytautas Ignas stained glass; post-closure the community gathered 3x/year. The former "Wooster OH" row was this same parish, misspelled — removed 2026-05-23.
Transitioned to non-Lithuanian Catholic use by 2017; interior heritage substantially preserved. Distinct from Worcester Šv. Kazimiero.

Our Lady of Vilnius, SoHo. The central ownership test case. Closed 2007 by Cardinal Egan; NY Court of Appeals ruled against parishioners 2011; building sold and demolished. Demolition-year conflict (2015 vs 2020) and sale-figure conflict flagged. No debt at closure.

Diocese did NOT close the parish; the community sold its own buildings during decline and moved to rented space. Parish formally still exists with monthly Lithuanian Mass; demographic attrition, not diocesan action.
Demolished ~2008; even a photograph could not be found online.
Closed; building sold by the diocese to a non-Roman Anglican body used as a cathedral. Lithuanian heritage markers preserved.
Closed ~May 2009 (Diocese of Albany); building intact as of 2019. Demographic collapse, no community to resist.
Sold; became a Syrian/Assyrian Catholic cathedral. Interior retained Lithuanian details.
Unnamed Lithuanian church; original building demolished after a 1981 fire; diocese refused repairs; congregation moved to the parish-hall basement; now Portuguese/Brazilian use.
Closed March 2 2014; church and rectory sold to Grace Gospel Church (Protestant). 102-year-old parish; first Lithuanian church 1912, current building 1961/62.
Closed quietly ~end of 2021, sold to a Spanish-speaking Pentecostal church. Survived review then closed. The Vanagas commemorative plaque was removed and not returned.
Closed ~2001; building sold ~2007 and converted to condos. Still apartments in 2023.
Unnamed Lithuanian church; closed and sold ~2015 to non-Catholics; Gediminas-pillars monument remains in the churchyard.
Closed Sept 30 2023 under the Hartford 2017 plan. First Lithuanian RC parish in New England (founded 1893/94). Survived the 2002 St. Patrick merger; a genuine grassroots revival did not protect it.
Closed; converted to apartments with commemorative plaques. Community noted Greek Orthodox parishes could not be closed because they are a separate religion.

Closed June 23 2013 under Together in Faith, merged into Dievo Apvaizdos Southfield. Survived a 1989 closure scare; resistance delayed but could not prevent closure. Only 34 registered parishioners at closure.
Closed 1995 when the pastor retired; contents shipped to Lithuania. Distinct from Detroit Šv. Antano and Detroit/Southfield Dievo Apvaizdos.

Closed/merged 2014 into Sts. Peter and Paul; sold to Spanish-speakers. Šv. Antano draugija founded 1900/01, church consecrated 1907. Survived an earlier ~2008 closure danger; ~$60K community savings deemed insufficient.
Closed Oct 18 2009, merged into the new consolidated Šv. Kazimiero; old building sold by diocese, now derelict. Buyer-identity conflict flagged.
Closed Oct 18 2009, merged with Šv. Jurgio into the new Šv. Kazimiero, which worships in the former DMNP building. Distinct from Detroit DMNP.

Closed 2006, demolished 2009/2010. Founding-year MAJOR CONFLICT: 1872, 1874, 1886, 1891 all cited; 1891 is the breakaway Šv. Jurgio parish, building 1894. The strongest thesis case: ~$1M treasury, credible $360K repair estimate, landmark status and a treaty, yet still demolished. Diocese took the ~$1M savings. Coal region.
Closed April 6 2008, merged into a new parish at St. John the Evangelist; later sold to a private buyer. Coal region.
Demolished 2015; site now a flat field. One of the two largest, most beautiful churches in the region. Coal region.
Demolished; the other of the two largest regional churches. Coal region.
Pittsburgh area. Built 1903; demolished pre-2017 after repeated arson; only the cornerstone survives. Not anthracite coal region.
Southern Pittsburgh. Closed 1992; converted to 12 luxury condominiums; sold by the diocese to private developers. Not anthracite coal region.

RC. Closed ~2009 in the Allentown/Scranton sweep; building given to convert-priests. The original Lithuanian RC church Scranton Lithuanians built then lost to the bishop through the courts; precipitating event of the LNCC schism. Coal region.
RC. Closed as a Lithuanian parish; now a Latin Mass community (Priestly Fraternity of St. Peter); ethnically transferred, not formally dissolved. Coal region.
Built 1914; closed ~2009 in the Allentown wave; building sold by the diocese to a private individual (R. Demyanovich, ~$24,000) to prevent warehouse use. Not a community buyout. Coal region.
Founded 1901 (rebuilt 1915); closed ~2001-2002 for mold/moisture; demolished March 2024. A 1915 time capsule with a Lithuanian letter was found in the foundations. Coal region.
Founded 1892; closed 1992, became part of Divine Redeemer Parish 1995. Home of the founding of the Sisters of St. Casimir (1907). School building tax-sold 2022. Coal region.
Closed 2008 under the Allentown plan; building sold; Jonynas stained glass rescued post-closure by the Šaknys project. Coal region.
Lithuanian-immigrant-built 1907; closed as a parish 2015 (merged into St. Charles Borromeo); officially closed as a worship site, final Mass Jan 5 2026. Coal region.
Closed; converted to a private residence (Lithuanian-descent owner); interior fully intact. Coal region.
Closed 2017 (the only Lithuanian church in Rhode Island); building sold to Seventh-day Adventists. Community declined a diocesan offer to bring in Latin American immigrants.

Survived inside the diocese, confirmed Jan 2026. The only PA Lithuanian parish with a Lithuanian-language Sunday Mass. The separately standing RECTORY was sold ~2023-24 for $1.2M to fund operations; church deed unchanged and still diocese-owned.

Suppressed to worship-site status July 2011; survives as an open worship site of the merged Philadelphia parish. Survived earlier reviews then was suppressed. Lithuanian-Mass continuity after 2011 is conflicted in the sources.

Merged into an American parish ~2015-2016, losing its Lithuanian identity. Survived multiple Philadelphia reviews then was merged out. Documented only in the Philadelphia Archdiocese plan table and Key Findings (e); no full case file in the document.

Survived inside the diocese; ~20-year campaign; 2021-22 merger deferred. Community ceded the deed to the archdiocese in 1904 in exchange for a priest. The only Lithuanian church in New England. Refused merger consent Feb 2022.

Survived inside the diocese; debt-free; on the National Register of Historic Places (the only such Lithuanian church in the corpus). Parish dedicated 1915; survived via demonstrated vitality and congregational diversification.

Consolidated parish constituted Oct 25 2009 from the merger of Šv. Jurgio Cleveland (1895) and DMNP Cleveland (1929). Survived inside the diocese; a 2014 financial crisis averted via a diocesan-approved school lease.

Marquette Park (Nativity BVM). Survived inside the diocese; by 2020 the sole surviving Lithuanian RC parish in Chicago. Chronic deficit covered by the archdiocese as a high-interest loan; building valued ~$20M and not parish-owned.

Brighton Park (Immaculate Conception). Merged 2019 with Five Holy Martyrs into a multi-ethnic entity; a Lithuanian RC presence and a separate Lithuanian Mass continue; both buildings open. Filed as standing/surviving.
Survived inside the diocese; small but active. Formal Lithuanian-parish status transferred to the Mexican community 2011, but Lithuanian Masses continued through at least Dec 2019.

RC. Detroit/Southfield. Survived inside the diocese; the sole surviving Lithuanian RC parish in metro Detroit; enrolled in Families of Parishes 2025. Founded 1908 as Šv. Jurgio. Distinct from Scranton LNCC and Chicago 18th-St. Dievo Apvaizdos.

RC ethnic mission established 1990; operates inside the community-owned Pasaulio Lietuvių Centras. Survived an RMC grouping. The venue is community-owned but the mission's canonical function remains diocesan.
Williamsburg (Annunciation BVM). Diocese of Brooklyn. Survived inside the diocese; major renovation completed 2019; from Sept 2025 hosts the displaced Maspeth Lithuanian-language Masses.

Queens. Diocese of Brooklyn. NOT closed: a closure letter was issued Feb 16 2025 but reversed Sept 12 2025 to limited-basis opening. Survived review then targeted. Status genuinely unresolved as of March 2026; no community buyout.
Church built 1895. Under financial/maintenance stress by 2022 (rectory sold, electrical fire); status uncertain, unresolved. One of the last Lithuanian churches in New Jersey.
Officially Lithuanian (a Lithuanian Church sign hangs); daily Masses; by 2022 one of the strongest Lithuanian bastions in New Jersey. Survived.
Diocese of Bridgeport. Now majority Spanish-speaking but Lithuanian Masses still held; interior remains very Lithuanian. Survived/transitioned, active 2023-24.

Diocese of Hartford. The surrounding Lithuanian neighborhood was demolished by urban renewal, but the church building stands and was active as of late 2023. 2017 hall-only access flagged as transient ambiguity.
The one Lithuanian church remaining under Catholic ownership in the entire 10-church northern PA cluster. Survived. Coal region.
Annunciation BVM. Lithuanian-community-built 1914; still open; hosted the 103rd Lietuvių Dienos festival 2017; adjacent Lithuanian museum since 1982. Survived. Coal region.
Third building 1951, community-funded but archdiocese-titled. Active cultural center; survived inside the Archdiocese of Los Angeles, confirmed 2020.
Founded ~1904. Survived; by 2024 described as the best-preserved living first-wave Lithuanian neighborhood in America.
Founded 1895. Diocese of Belleville. Survived against extreme odds (11 of 13 East St. Louis Catholic churches closed); landmark-designated; still officially Lithuanian after 126+ years.
Distinct from the closed Šv. Juozapo Waterbury. Survived; still functioning 2022; temporarily storing the Frackville/Shenandoah-area Lithuanian Museum's artifacts.
Transitioned / de facto closed as a Lithuanian parish; building used by an American parish; one token Sunday Lithuanian Mass with no Lithuanian priest.

Providence of God. LITHUANIAN NATIONAL CATHOLIC, lay-governed, independent of Rome; community-owned. The last surviving officially Lithuanian parish in the entire NE PA coal region; survives precisely because no bishop can close or sell it. Building 1915. Coal region. Distinct from Detroit/Southfield RC and Chicago 18th-St. RC Dievo Apvaizdos.

LITHUANIAN NATIONAL CATHOLIC, community-owned. Also called Šv. Marijos Tautinė Katalikų Parapija. Closed and sold 2002 BY THE COMMUNITY at parishioners' request; proceeds went to the community's own LNCC cemetery fund. Contrast with RC Šv. Pranciškaus Lawrence same year.

LITHUANIAN NATIONAL CATHOLIC, community-governed, never diocesan. Broke from RC in the 1916 schism; building sold 1972 and demolished; ~30-40 parishioners at closure. Parish cemetery at Bensalem PA survives.

LITHUANIAN NATIONAL CATHOLIC, community-governed. Closed ~1969 and demolished; bell and cornerstone made into a 2004 memorial; cemetery maintained. Independence removed diocesan risk but not demographic risk.

Lithuanian Evangelical Lutheran, congregationally governed, community-owned (bought its Marquette Park building in 1957). Voted to sell and relocate twice (Western Springs, then Darien Dec 2019); thriving and growing. Self-owned comparator.
Independent self-governing Lithuanian Lutheran congregation, founded by a knygnešys from Tauragė. Survived; small, aging, still active as of 2019. Independent of any diocese.

CANADA - out-of-scope comparator, NOT counted in the U.S. total of 83. Quebec QC. Under Quebec civil law the parish (not the diocese) is the juridical owner; the diocese cannot act unilaterally. Survived; aging. Natural-experiment comparator.
CANADA - out-of-scope comparator, NOT counted in the U.S. total of 83. Quebec QC. Quebec parish-ownership law; diocese cannot act unilaterally. Survived; aging. Natural-experiment comparator.

CANADA - out-of-scope comparator, NOT counted in the U.S. total of 83. Ontario ON. Closed October 2020 after 61 years; the community explicitly framed the closure as self-determination, contrasting it with forced U.S. diocesan closures.
